# Extractor Receipt

## Summary

This source contains controlled empirical evidence about how single-agent and multi-agent LLM systems perform across four agentic benchmarks, including quantitative results on architecture effects, coordination costs, error propagation, predictive modeling, and task-dependent performance limits.

## Evidence Files

### file: evidence-01.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-02-025
evidence_id: evidence-01
kind: method
citation: Kim et al., Towards a Science of Scaling Agent Systems, Abstract
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.08296
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The study evaluates 180 controlled agent-system configurations spanning five architectures, three LLM families, and four benchmarks.

## Source Excerpt

“We evaluate this across four diverse benchmarks... Using five canonical agent architectures... instantiated across three LLM families, we perform a controlled evaluation spanning 180 configurations, standardizing tools, prompt structures, and token budgets to isolate architectural effects from implementation confounds.”

## Note

The source describes the experiment as controlled by matching tools, prompts, and token budgets across configurations.
```

### file: evidence-02.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-02-025
evidence_id: evidence-02
kind: metric
citation: Kim et al., Towards a Science of Scaling Agent Systems, Abstract
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.08296
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The paper’s predictive model achieves cross-validated R^2 = 0.513 on unseen held-out data.

## Source Excerpt

“We derive a predictive model using empirical coordination metrics... that achieves cross-validated R^2 = 0.513.”

## Note

The metric is reported for the predictive model built from empirical coordination measures.
```

### file: evidence-03.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-02-025
evidence_id: evidence-03
kind: metric
citation: Kim et al., Towards a Science of Scaling Agent Systems, Introduction
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.08296
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Leave-one-domain-out cross-validation achieves R^2 = 0.89.

## Source Excerpt

“Critically, this framework generalizes beyond training configurations: leave-one-domain-out cross-validation achieves R^2 = 0.89.”

## Note

This metric is presented as evidence of cross-domain generalization beyond the training configurations.
```

### file: evidence-04.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-02-025
evidence_id: evidence-04
kind: result
citation: Kim et al., Towards a Science of Scaling Agent Systems, Abstract
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.08296
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
The framework predicts the optimal coordination strategy for 87% of held-out configurations.

## Source Excerpt

“The framework predicts the optimal coordination strategy for 87% of held-out configurations.”

## Note

The prediction target is architecture selection across held-out configurations.
```

### file: evidence-05.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-02-025
evidence_id: evidence-05
kind: result
citation: Kim et al., Towards a Science of Scaling Agent Systems, Abstract
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.08296
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
Once single-agent baselines exceed about 45%, added coordination shows diminishing or negative returns (β = -0.408, p < 0.001).

## Source Excerpt

“We observe that coordination yields diminishing or negative returns (β = −0.408, p < 0.001) once single-agent baselines exceed an empirical threshold of ∼45%.”

## Note

This is a thresholded empirical pattern, not a universal law; it introduces a tension against the claim that adding agents generally helps.
```

### file: evidence-06.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-02-025
evidence_id: evidence-06
kind: result
citation: Kim et al., Towards a Science of Scaling Agent Systems, Abstract
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.08296
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
Tool-heavy tasks suffer disproportionately from multi-agent coordination overhead under fixed computational budgets (β = -0.330, p < 0.001).

## Source Excerpt

“We identify three dominant effects: (1) a tool-coordination trade-off: under fixed computational budgets, tool-heavy tasks suffer disproportionately from multi-agent overhead.”

## Note

The evidence identifies an empirical trade-off and directly limits claims that multi-agent coordination scales well on tool-rich tasks.
```

### file: evidence-07.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-02-025
evidence_id: evidence-07
kind: metric
citation: Kim et al., Towards a Science of Scaling Agent Systems, Abstract
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.08296
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: direct
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
Independent multi-agent systems amplify errors 17.2×, while centralized coordination contains error amplification to 4.4×.

## Source Excerpt

“Independent agents amplify errors 17.2× through unchecked propagation, while centralized coordination contains this to 4.4×.”

## Note

The source explicitly attributes the difference to unchecked propagation versus containment through centralized coordination.
```

### file: evidence-08.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-02-025
evidence_id: evidence-08
kind: result
citation: Kim et al., Towards a Science of Scaling Agent Systems, Abstract
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.08296
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
On financial reasoning tasks, centralized coordination improves performance by 80.9%.

## Source Excerpt

“Centralized coordination improves performance by 80.9% on parallelizable tasks like financial reasoning.”

## Note

The source presents this as a benchmark-specific relative improvement.
```

### file: evidence-09.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-02-025
evidence_id: evidence-09
kind: result
citation: Kim et al., Towards a Science of Scaling Agent Systems, Abstract
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.08296
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
On dynamic web navigation, decentralized coordination gains 9.2% relative to single-agent performance, while centralized coordination gains 0.2%.

## Source Excerpt

“Decentralized coordination excels on dynamic web navigation (+9.2% vs. +0.2%).”

## Note

The source contrasts decentralized and centralized performance on the same task type.
```

### file: evidence-10.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-02-025
evidence_id: evidence-10
kind: result
citation: Kim et al., Towards a Science of Scaling Agent Systems, Abstract
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.08296
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
For sequential reasoning tasks, every tested multi-agent variant reduced performance by 39% to 70%.

## Source Excerpt

“Yet for sequential reasoning tasks, every multi-agent variant we tested degraded performance by 39–70%.”

## Note

This is a direct negative result and should remain attached as a caveat against generalizing multi-agent gains across task structures.
```

### file: evidence-11.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-02-025
evidence_id: evidence-11
kind: metric
citation: Kim et al., Towards a Science of Scaling Agent Systems, Section 4.4
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.08296
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: bounded
contradiction_relevance: tension
---
Total reasoning turns scale with agent count as T = 2.72 × (n + 0.5)^1.724 with R^2 = 0.974.

## Source Excerpt

“Total reasoning turns... exhibit power-law growth with agent count: T = 2.72 × (n + 0.5)^1.724, R^2 = 0.974... This super-linear exponent (1.724 > 1) reflects quadratic message complexity...”

## Note

The source presents this as super-linear growth in coordination burden, which bounds practical team scaling under fixed budgets.
```

### file: evidence-12.md
```markdown
---
version: evidence.v1
article_id: art-2026-03-02-025
evidence_id: evidence-12
kind: metric
citation: Kim et al., Towards a Science of Scaling Agent Systems, Table 5
source_url: https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.08296
confidence: high
mechanism_relevance: none
contradiction_relevance: none
---
Success per 1K tokens is 67.7 for single-agent systems, 42.4 for independent MAS, 23.9 for decentralized MAS, 21.5 for centralized MAS, and 13.6 for hybrid MAS.

## Source Excerpt

“Table 5... Success/1K tokens 67.7 42.4 23.9 21.5 13.6” for “SAS Independent Decentralized Centralized Hybrid.”

## Note

These values are reported under matched total reasoning-token budgets across systems.
---

Once single-agent baselines exceed about 45%, added coordination shows diminishing or negative returns (β = -0.408, p < 0.001).

## Source Excerpt

## Note

```
